messages: I accepted an offer last week and will be changing jobs soon; I want to talk through a plan for the transition.
The new role starts in about six weeks and will require an interstate move. The company offered to cover relocation and some travel for onboarding.
At my current job I'm lead on two major projects and the team is small—I'm worried about leaving them short-staffed. How should I approach the handover?
I'm pondering whether to give the standard two-week notice or more because of handover complexity. Any pros/cons to extend my notice?
The offer included a signing bonus and a bump in pay. If my manager makes a counteroffer, is it okay to discuss total compensation openly, or would that look tacky?
Also concerned about announcing this on LinkedIn—when is the right time to update it so I don't accidentally tip off my current employer?
Logistics are tricky: lease end dates, movers, spouse job prospects. What timeline should I use to minimize overlap and stress?
The new company mentioned flexibility in the first few months. Should I try to get that in writing, or is verbal okay until I'm on payroll?
I want to hit the ground running in the first 90 days. What should be my top three priorities to establish credibility?
Health insurance is a concern—how do most people avoid a gap between plans when switching employers?
I have some accrued vacation. Is it normal to cash some out instead of taking it during a notice period? What are potential tax or policy implications?
My current role involves a lot of undocumented tribal knowledge. How much detail do I need in handover docs so the team can operate without me?
If my current employer asks me to delay my start date here for a leadership handoff, is that a red flag or a reasonable request?
Should I arrange an exit meeting to share constructive feedback, or will that be awkward so soon after giving notice?
I want to avoid burning bridges. Any wording suggestions for a resignation letter that strikes a professional tone?
If I explicitly offer to help train my replacement, is it better to do that in person or via recorded sessions and docs?
My spouse may not find work right away at the new location. Am I justified in asking HR for a slightly later start to accommodate their job search?
If the company agrees to delay my start, what are reasonable expectations about compensation and benefits during the gap?
Are there any pitfalls to rolling over a 401(k) immediately when switching, or should I wait until I'm actually on the new plan?
Who should I tell first among colleagues—close teammates, mentors, or broader department? Which order minimizes awkwardness?
During my notice period, how do I decline new assignments tactfully without appearing unhelpful?
I want a realistic moving budget. Even with employer support, what hidden costs should I expect when moving out of state?
If I negotiate a later start date, what reasons are usually acceptable to employers beyond 'logistics'?
Last practical question for now: should I hold off on major purchases tied to the move (like a new car) until everything is confirmed?

Update: I just got an email from the incoming company — they're putting new hires on hold for the near term and have paused their moving assistance. They say they still want me, but can't confirm a firm start date or relocation support right now.
To complicate things, I already handed in my resignation yesterday; my last day is scheduled in four weeks. I'm suddenly wondering whether to try to retract it. What should I consider?
My manager has already started reallocating work and I've got a candidate in mind to train. If I try to stay, will that look unstable, or can it be handled professionally?
If I press the new employer to either let me start remotely or pay for the move cancellation, how should I phrase that without sounding confrontational?
We already booked a moving company and paid deposits on a rental; cancelling would be costly. How do I approach getting reimbursed for cancel fees given their pause on the package?
Should I go ahead and start any paperwork for benefits and payroll with the new employer in case they're able to onboard me remotely, or would that be premature?
My spouse had given notice at their job to focus on the move. Now they're in a bind. How do we coordinate next steps at home while waiting on the new employer?
From a career perspective, is it safer to stay put and try to rescind the resignation, or to insist the new employer honor the original timeline some other way?
If the company won't restore the relocation package but still wants me, is it reasonable to ask for an increased signing bonus to cover our moving losses?
I'm concerned about how this looks on my resume if I delay or never start—any ways to frame the timeline so it doesn't hurt long-term prospects?

I need an immediate, prioritized action plan for the next two weeks that assumes the offer is still live but onboarding and moving support are suspended. Start with what to do today, then week by week.

Also draft two concise scripts: one I can use to ask my current boss if I can withdraw my resignation without awkwardness, and another to send to incoming HR to request either a remote start or compensation for cancellation costs. Keep each under five sentences.

Give me a short checklist (no more than eight items) prioritizing: protecting cash/payments we've already made, preserving health and retirement coverage, and minimizing reputational risk with both employers.

Provide two quick negotiation lines to open and close conversations with the new employer: one focused on covering out-of-pocket move expenses and one focused on deferring physical relocation while starting remotely.
Give two brief lines for the current manager conversation if they push back on me staying—phrases that preserve goodwill and keep future options open.
Finally, recommend a conservative follow-up schedule to touch base with the incoming company over the next few months without seeming needy—how often and in what tone should I reach out?
